era_context: The Roman Tetrarchy under Diocletian and Maximian is at the height of its power and organization. Diocletian initiates the Great Persecution of Christians, marking a major shift in imperial religious policy. In China, the Western Jin Dynasty is in the midst of the War of the Eight Princes. The Sassanid Persian Empire under Narses I is in its final years.

events.title: Diocletian Issues Edict Against Christians
events.description: In 303 CE, Roman Emperor Diocletian issued an edict ordering the destruction of Christian churches, the burning of Christian texts, and the removal of Christians from positions of authority. This marked the beginning of the Great Persecution, the most severe and systematic persecution of Christians in the Roman Empire. The edict was issued at Diocletian's palace in Nicomedia (in Bithynia, modern-day Turkey) and was enforced with varying intensity across the empire, most severely in the Eastern provinces under Galerius's jurisdiction. The persecution would continue until 311 CE and produced numerous Christian martyrs whose acts were later recorded in hagiographic literature.
key_figures: Maximian (enforcer in the West, though less rigorously)
Constantius Chlorus (Caesar in the West, reportedly less enthusiastic about enforcement)

sources.name: Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors

events.certainty_note: The edict of 303 CE is one of the most well-documented events of this period, attested in multiple near-contemporary and later Christian sources. The date and basic facts are not disputed.

events.title: War of the Eight Princes Continues in Western Jin China
events.description: The War of the Eight Princes, which began in 291 CE, continued throughout 303 CE. This civil conflict between imperial princes over succession and control of the Jin court was devastating the empire. Major military campaigns were fought across northern and central China, with armies commanded by various princes competing for power. The war caused widespread destruction, displaced populations, and depleted state resources. By 303, the Jin central government had lost effective control over much of the empire, creating conditions for the subsequent invasion of northern China by non-Han peoples.
key_figures: Emperor Hui of Jin (nominal ruler, mentally incapacitated)
Various princes competing for power (Sima Lun, Sima Ying, Sima Wei, and others)
events.certainty_note: The War of the Eight Princes is well-documented in Chinese historical records. The year 303 falls within the confirmed period of the conflict.

events.title: Narses I of Sassanid Persia Dies
events.description: King Narses I of the Sassanid Empire died in 302 or 303 CE (sources vary on the exact year). Narses I had ruled for approximately nine years (r. 293–302/303 CE) and had pursued military campaigns against the Romans and internal consolidation. His death marked the end of a period of relative stability and military success. The succession was contested among the Sassanid nobility, with the great noble families competing for influence over the throne. This succession dispute weakened Sassanid authority and contributed to a period of internal instability that would last for several decades.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Sassanid chronology for this period is uncertain. The death of Narses I is dated variously to 302–303 CE in different sources. The exact year cannot be determined with precision from available sources.

events.title: Earthquake in Antioch and Eastern Mediterranean
events.description: A major earthquake struck Antioch and the surrounding regions of the Eastern Mediterranean in 303 CE. The earthquake caused significant damage to cities and infrastructure, particularly in Syria and Palestine. Antioch, one of the largest cities in the Roman Empire, suffered considerable destruction. The earthquake is recorded in several ancient sources and is sometimes associated with the beginning of the Great Persecution, as some Christian sources interpreted it as divine judgment. The earthquake contributed to the economic and social disruption of the region during this period.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from excavations in Antioch and other Eastern Mediterranean sites

events.certainty_note: The earthquake is attested in multiple ancient sources, but the exact date (303 CE) is not universally agreed upon. Some sources place it slightly earlier or later. Archaeological evidence supports the occurrence of a major seismic event in this period.
cross_references: 303-001 (Edict of Diocletian, sometimes associated with the earthquake)

disconfirming_evidence: The Great Persecution is sometimes portrayed as a unified, empire-wide campaign, but modern scholarship emphasizes significant regional variation. In the Western provinces under Maximian and Constantius Chlorus, enforcement was often less rigorous than in the East under Galerius. Some Christian communities negotiated compromises or went underground rather than facing martyrdom. Additionally, the edict of 303 was not the first imperial action against Christians; earlier emperors had issued edicts, and the 303 edict was more systematic and severe than its predecessors. The earthquake in Antioch is sometimes dated to 303 CE, but some sources place it in 300 CE or 306 CE, and the exact date remains uncertain.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 303 CE is rich for the Roman Empire, with multiple near-contemporary and later sources providing detailed accounts of the edict and the beginning of the Great Persecution. Christian sources are particularly abundant, though they are often hagiographic and may exaggerate the scope or intensity of persecution. Chinese sources provide good documentation of the War of the Eight Princes. Sassanid sources are limited and mediated through later Islamic historians. The perspective is predominantly elite and institutional; the experiences of ordinary people are largely absent from the record. The earthquake is documented in ancient sources but with some uncertainty about the exact date.

graph_edges.note: The edict of 303 directly led to the continuation and intensification of Christian persecution in subsequent years.
The edict of 303 marks the beginning of the Great Persecution (303–311 CE).
The War of the Eight Princes in 303 directly contributed to the conditions that enabled the invasion of northern China.
The earthquake in Antioch occurred around the same time as the edict of Diocletian, and some sources associate the two events.
